items.title: B19V Genotypes and Epidemiology
items.content: B19V, a member of the Erythroparvovirus genus, has three genotypes (1, 2, and 3) with similar properties. Genotype 1 is predominant globally, while genotype 2 persists in older individuals without causing active infections. Genotype 3 is more common in western Africa.
keyPoints: B19V belongs to the genus Erythroparvovirus.
Three genotypes: 1 (predominant worldwide), 2 (persistent but rarely causes active infection), and 3 (more common in western Africa).
Infection rates approach 50% within schools, day-care centers, and households.
By age 15, ~50% of children have detectable IgG antibody to B19V; seroprevalence may rise to 80% among the elderly.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · Parvovirus Infections | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission and Outbreak Patterns of B19V
items.content: B19V outbreaks, causing childhood rash (erythema infectiousum), occur in schools and day-care centers during winter and spring. Transmission is primarily respiratory, occurring before symptoms like rash or arthralgia appear.
keyPoints: Outbreaks cause erythema infectiousum, common in schools/day-care centers.
Transmission occurs via the respiratory route before symptom onset.
Infection rates can reach 50% in close-contact settings.
Viral load can be extremely high (~10^14 particles/mL) in patients with hemolytic disorders or compromised immune systems.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · Parvovirus Infections | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanism of B19V Infection and Impact on Erythropoiesis
items.content: B19 virus (B19V) targets erythroid progenitors, replicating due to the presence of specific receptors. This results in high-titer viremia with $10^{4-12}$ virus particles/mL at peak infection. The cessation of red cell production occurs due to virus-induced cytotoxicity, leading to transient anemia in immunocompetent individuals and potentially severe or chronic anemia in those with increased erythropoiesis or inability to mount a neutralizing antibody response.
keyPoints: B19V replicates in erythroid progenitors.
High-titer viremia: $10^{4-12}$ virus particles/mL detectable at peak infection.
Cessation of red cell production due to cytotoxicity.
Transient anemia in immunocompetent individuals with normal hemopoiesis.
Severe or chronic anemia risk in those with increased erythropoiesis or inability to produce neutralizing antibodies.

items.title: Persistent B19V Infection and Potential Disease Associations
items.content: B19V can enter nonpermissive cells via antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) and persist in tissues like the heart, liver, kidneys, synovia, brain, and bones. While generally not affecting health, this persistent presence may contribute to disease presentations such as myocarditis in predisposed individuals.
keyPoints: B19V can enter nonpermissive cells via ADE.
Persistent B19V DNA presence in tissues like heart, liver, kidneys, synovia, brain, and bones.
Potential association with diseases like myocarditis in predisposed individuals.

items.title: Erythema Infectiosum Presentation and Management
items.content: Erythema infectiosum, also known as fifth disease or slapped-cheek disease, is the main manifestation of symptomatic B19V infection. It typically begins with a minor febrile prodrome 7-10 days post-exposure, followed by a sudden facial rash that spreads in a lacy pattern to other body parts. The rash can recur with exercise or sunbathing but does not indicate infectiousness.
keyPoints: Main manifestation of symptomatic B19V infection is erythema infectiosum.
Febrile prodrome occurs ~7-10 days after exposure, often absent.
Facial rash develops suddenly and spreads in a lacy pattern.
Rash recurs with exercise or sunbathing but child is no longer infectious.

items.title: Transient Aplastic Crisis in Hemolytic Disorders
items.content: B19V infection can cause a transient aplastic crisis (TAC) in patients with hemolytic disorders, leading to severe anemia and low reticulocyte count. This is characterized by giant pronormoblasts in bone marrow but resolves as the cytopathic effect ceases, resulting in lifelong immunity.
keyPoints: B19V causes transient aplastic crisis (TAC) in hemolytic disorders.
Patients present with severe anemia and low reticulocyte count.
Bone marrow shows giant pronormoblasts and absence of erythroid precursors.
Anemia resolves with cessation of cytopathic infection, leading to lifelong immunity.

items.title: Hydrops Fetalis Risk in B19V Infection During Pregnancy
items.content: B19V infection during pregnancy can lead to hydrops fetalis and fetal loss, with a ~30% risk of transplacental infection. The excess risk of fetal loss is ~9% if the mother is infected before 20 weeks gestation. Monitoring and potential intrauterine transfusions are crucial for managing affected pregnancies.
keyPoints: B19V can cause hydrops fetalis and/or fetal loss during pregnancy.
~30% risk of transplacental fetal infection.
Excess risk of fetal loss is ~9% if infected before 20 weeks gestation.
Monitoring and intrauterine transfusions may be necessary.

items.title: Diagnosing Acute Parvovirus B19 Infection
items.content: Acute parvovirus B19 infection is diagnosed through serological tests detecting IgM and IgG antibodies. IgM appears at the time of rash or by the third day in hematologic disorders, while IgG becomes detectable by the seventh day and persists for life. Seroconversion or a fourfold increase in IgG titer over two weeks confirms acute infection.
keyPoints: IgM detected by indirect EIA at time of rash or by third day of TAC
IgG detectable by seventh day, persists lifelong
Seroconversion or >4-fold increase in IgG titer diagnostic for acute infection
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Parvovirus B19 Infection | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Utilizing PCR and Serology to Differentiate Acute from Past B19V Infection
items.content: PCR can detect high levels of B19V DNA during acute infection, with >10¹² IU/mL in serum. Quantitative PCR is necessary as viral DNA remains detectable for months or years post-infection. High avidity and linear epitope-specific IgG indicate past infection, while low-avidity IgG suggests recent exposure.
keyPoints: >10¹² B19V DNA IU/mL detected during acute viremia
Quantitative PCR needed due to prolonged detectability of viral DNA
High avidity and linear epitope-specific IgG indicate past infection
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Parvovirus B19 Infection | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Which genotype of parvovirus B19 is currently predominant worldwide?
items.explanation: Genotype 1 is currently the predominant genotype of parvovirus B19 worldwide.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · Parvovirus Infections | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary route of transmission for parvovirus B19?
items.explanation: Transmission of parvovirus B19 occurs primarily via the respiratory route.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · Parvovirus Infections | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: In which settings are outbreaks of B19V infection, causing childhood rash, most common?
items.explanation: Outbreaks of B19V infection causing childhood rash are most common in schools and day-care centers.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · Parvovirus Infections | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which receptor is recognized by the N-terminal unique region of B19-virus protein 1 (VP1u) and contributes to its specificity in erythroid progenitors?
items.explanation: The N-terminal unique region of B19-virus protein 1 (VP1u) recognizes the yet unknown primary B19V receptor, contributing to its specificity in erythroid progenitors.

items.question: What is the role of the blood group P antigen in B19 virus infection?
options: It is recognized by VP1u and initiates viral replication.
It is necessary at a later intracellular step for infection.
It prevents the virus from entering erythroid progenitors.
It induces neutralizing antibodies against the virus.
items.explanation: The blood group P antigen, recognized by VP2, is needed at a later intracellular step in B19 virus infection.

items.question: What happens to erythropoiesis in immunocompetent individuals after acute B19V infection?
options: It causes permanent cessation of red cell production.
It is transient with minimal drop in hemoglobin levels.
It leads to severe anemia without recovery.
items.explanation: In immunocompetent individuals, the arrest of erythropoiesis after acute B19V infection is transient, with only a minimal drop in hemoglobin levels.

items.question: What is the classic facial rash associated with erythema infectiosum called?
items.explanation: Erythema infectiosum is also known as slapped-cheek disease or fifth disease.

items.question: In which group of patients does B19V cause a transient aplastic crisis (TAC)?
items.explanation: B19V is the predominant cause of TAC in individuals with hemolytic disorders, among other conditions.

items.question: What symptom may recur for weeks when exercising or sunbathing in children with erythema infectiosum?
items.explanation: The rash associated with erythema infectiosum may recur for weeks when exercising or sunbathing.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by persistent anemia and high titers of B19V DNA in immunocompromised patients?
items.explanation: Pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) is characterized by persistent anemia and high titers of B19V DNA in immunocompromised patients.

items.question: Which serological marker is considered diagnostic for acute B19V infection when comparing serum samples taken two weeks apart?
options: Seroconversion or a fourfold increase in IgG titer
Presence of B19V DNA by PCR
items.explanation: Seroconversion or a fourfold increase in IgG titer between serum samples taken two weeks apart is diagnostic for acute infection.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Parvovirus B19 Infection | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What method can differentiate between acute and past B19V infections by measuring the quality of IgG?
options: Denaturing EIA to measure IgG avidity
Detection of B19V DNA in serum by PCR
Epitope-type-specific (ETS) EIA for linear VP2 epitopes
items.explanation: Denaturing EIA measures the increasing avidity of IgG over time, helping to differentiate between acute and past infections.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Parvovirus B19 Infection | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Which diagnostic method is particularly useful in pregnancy or chronic anemia for detecting B19V infection?
options: Detection of B19V DNA by PCR
items.explanation: PCR detection of B19V DNA in serum (or amniotic fluid) is particularly useful in pregnancy or chronic anemia.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Parvovirus B19 Infection | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Genotype 2 of parvovirus B19 rarely causes active infections but can persist in tissues.
items.explanation: Genotype 2 rarely causes active infections but remains persistent in tissues of older individuals.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · Parvovirus Infections | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Plasma pools are not screened for B19V DNA and high-titer pools are used.
items.explanation: Plasma pools are now screened for B19V DNA, and high-titer pools are discarded.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · Parvovirus Infections | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Individuals lacking the P antigen are naturally resistant to B19V infection.
items.explanation: The absence of the P antigen makes individuals naturally resistant to B19V infection.

items.statement: B19V DNAemia in nonacute infections is due solely to encapsidated virus particles.
items.explanation: Nonacute B19V DNAemia is due to nonencapsidated naked DNA released from injured tissues, not just encapsidated virus particles.

items.statement: B19V infection typically causes a teratogenic effect leading to congenital abnormalities.
items.explanation: B19V does not appear to be teratogenic, though rare cases of CNS abnormalities and congenital anemia have been reported.

items.statement: The presence of B19V DNA in synovia alone is sufficient to prove a causative relation with arthropathy.
items.explanation: The mere presence of B19V DNA in synovia is not enough to prove a causative relation, as it can also be found in healthy individuals.

items.statement: A vaccine for the prevention of B19V infection has been approved and is widely used.
items.explanation: No vaccine has been approved for the prevention of B19V infection.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Parvovirus B19 Infection | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: IVIG is beneficial in treating immune-mediated erythema infectiousum caused by B19V.
items.explanation: Administration of IVIG is not beneficial for the immune-mediated erythema infectiousum.
items.reference: Parvovirus Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Parvovirus B19 Infection | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: B19V can remain dormant in multiple tissues after entering nonpermissive cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and ADE is a mechanism by which B19V enters nonpermissive cells and remains dormant.

items.assertion: B19V infection during pregnancy can lead to hydrops fetalis.
items.reason: B19V causes nonimmune hydrops, characterized by gross edema and severe anemia in the fetus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why B19V infection can lead to hydrops fetalis.

items.assertion: B19V DNA detection by PCR in tissues is clinically significant for diagnosing active infection.
items.reason: B19V DNA can be detected by PCR for life in many tissues, but this finding has no known clinical significance.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because B19V DNA detection by PCR in tissues is not clinically significant for diagnosing active infection.
